title: Improving Language Model Pretraining with Text Structure Information

structured.summary: This paper investigates the impact of surface-level text complexity reduction on language model pretraining and downstream performance by creating a parallel simplified corpus using LLaMA 3.1 8B. It finds that simpler text preserves core content and yields comparable fine-tuning results across model sizes, while complexity benefits zero-shot tasks involving world knowledge and entity tracking.

strengths.point: Controlled experimental setup isolating text complexity effects
strengths.evidence: Use of parallel corpora differing only in surface complexity, with semantic similarity around 79%, ensures core content preservation.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation across model sizes and multiple downstream tasks
strengths.evidence: Trained models from 28M to 500M parameters and evaluated on GLUE, SuperGLUE, and zero-shot multiple-choice benchmarks covering linguistic knowledge, entity/world knowledge, and commonsense reasoning.

strengths.point: Insightful findings on interaction between model size and text complexity
strengths.evidence: Smaller models benefit more from simplified text in perplexity, while larger models show minimal difference; zero-shot performance varies by task type and data complexity.

strengths.point: Practical implications for data curation and synthetic data generation
strengths.evidence: Shows surface-level diversity is less critical for fine-tuning but important for zero-shot generalization, guiding tailored dataset design.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to prior simplification and curriculum learning work
weaknesses.evidence: No discussion or benchmarking against existing text simplification methods or curriculum learning approaches limits novelty claims.

weaknesses.point: Simplification quality and hallucination risk not systematically evaluated
weaknesses.evidence: No human evaluation or error analysis to verify that LLaMA-based simplifications preserve factual correctness and avoid hallucinations.

weaknesses.point: Training limited to one epoch and relatively small dataset size
weaknesses.evidence: Models trained only for one epoch on ~2B tokens, which may be insufficient for convergence and limits conclusions about scaling effects.

weaknesses.point: Sparse details on zero-shot evaluation setup and reproducibility
weaknesses.evidence: Missing prompt templates, answer selection methods, hyperparameters, and simplification prompt details hinder reproducibility.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation restricted to causal language models and classification tasks
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments on encoder or encoder-decoder models, nor on generative or open-ended tasks, limiting generalizability.

weaknesses.point: No statistical significance testing reported for performance differences
weaknesses.evidence: Small observed differences lack confidence intervals or hypothesis testing, making it unclear if results are meaningful.

weaknesses.point: Limited linguistic analysis of which phenomena are affected by simplification
weaknesses.evidence: No breakdown of effects on coreference, negation, syntactic complexity, or discourse-level phenomena.

questions.question: How does varying degrees of simplification impact model performance?
questions.motivation: Understanding whether mild versus aggressive simplification differentially affects learning could inform optimal data preprocessing.

questions.question: Would similar findings hold for encoder-only or encoder-decoder architectures?
questions.motivation: Extending analysis beyond causal LMs is important for broader applicability to popular models like BERT or T5.

questions.question: How do simplification-induced hallucinations or factual distortions affect downstream tasks?
questions.motivation: Ensuring factual integrity is critical for reliable model behavior, especially in knowledge-intensive applications.

questions.question: Can training for more epochs or on larger datasets change the interaction between model size and text complexity?
questions.motivation: Longer training and more data might reveal different scaling behaviors and better isolate complexity effects.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to prior simplification and curriculum learning work. No discussion or benchmarking against existing text simplification methods or curriculum learning approaches limits novelty claims
critique_points.point_002: Simplification quality and hallucination risk not systematically evaluated. No human evaluation or error analysis to verify that LLaMA-based simplifications preserve factual correctness and avoid hallucinations
critique_points.point_003: Training limited to one epoch and relatively small dataset size. Models trained only for one epoch on ~2B tokens, which may be insufficient for convergence and limits conclusions about scaling effects
critique_points.point_004: Sparse details on zero-shot evaluation setup and reproducibility. Missing prompt templates, answer selection methods, hyperparameters, and simplification prompt details hinder reproducibility
critique_points.point_005: Evaluation restricted to causal language models and classification tasks. No experiments on encoder or encoder-decoder models, nor on generative or open-ended tasks, limiting generalizability
critique_points.point_006: No statistical significance testing reported for performance differences. Small observed differences lack confidence intervals or hypothesis testing, making it unclear if results are meaningful
critique_points.point_007: Limited linguistic analysis of which phenomena are affected by simplification. No breakdown of effects on coreference, negation, syntactic complexity, or discourse-level phenomena
